metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether `lizard>=1.23` is consumed as a Python library (in-process `import lizard` in `analyzer.py`) or purely to provision the `lizard` CLI binary, and confirm whether the new path replaces or coexists with the existing CLI shell-out in `engine_b_backends.py`.
evidence: `engine_b_backends.py:337`: 'scc/lizard are NOT installed here' — lizard treated as external CLI binary via `shutil.which`
`scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py:49-50`: shells to `['lizard', tree]` via subprocess, no `import lizard`
`docs/experiments/code-grounding-spike/spike2_derisk.py:50`: only POC uses `import lizard` in-process
Plan text: 'scc and jscpd are EXTERNAL CLIs (not pip deps) detected at runtime' — but then lists `lizard>=1.23` as a pip dep, creating ambiguity about lizard's intended usage mode
findings.finding: The plan adds `lizard>=1.23` as a pip dependency for in-process import inside `metrics/analyzer.py`, but the existing codebase treats lizard exclusively as an external CLI (subprocess shell-outs in `engine_b_backends.py` and `scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py`). Only the experiment POC (`docs/experiments/code-grounding-spike/spike2_derisk.py`) uses `import lizard` in-process. The plan does not clarify whether the `[metrics]` extra uses lizard as a pip-installed Python library (new in-process usage mode) or merely to provision its CLI binary — and whether the new in-process import path in `analyzer.py` is intended to coexist with or supersede the CLI shelling in `engine_b_backends.py`.
findings.location: AC line 1 (`lizard>=1.23`); Description (`scc and jscpd are EXTERNAL CLIs … detected at runtime`)
scenarios: If lizard is pip-installed just for its CLI binary, `guard_import('lizard', extra='metrics')` inside `analyzer.py` would succeed without `[metrics]` on many systems (lizard CLI is sometimes installed globally), creating a false-positive guard.

If lizard is imported in-process, the new `analyzer.py` path and the existing `engine_b_backends.py` CLI path become two separate integration points for the same tool — a potential duplication the plan should address.

evidence: vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific `lizard` Python API entry point(s) `analyzer.py` will call (e.g. `lizard.analyze_file`, the return type, and the complexity field) so the in-process surface is pinned and testable — the existing spike only proved bare importability, not API shape.
evidence: All existing lizard usage in repo is CLI subprocess: `engine_b_backends.py` shells to `which lizard`/`scc`; `scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py:49-50` runs `subprocess.run(['lizard', tree])`
`docs/experiments/code-grounding-spike/spike2_derisk.py:50`: only POC is `import lizard  # noqa` — bare importability probe, no API call
Plan description: 'scc and jscpd are EXTERNAL CLIs (not pip deps)' but then names `lizard>=1.23` as pip dep — lizard is being elevated from CLI to in-process library without naming the API surface
No repo file calls `lizard.analyze_file`, `lizard.get_extensions`, or any lizard Python API function
Evidence class: Partially-verified (library exists on PyPI and is importable; specific in-process API surface unconfirmed by repo evidence)
findings.finding: The plan introduces lizard as an in-process Python library import (`guard_import('lizard', extra='metrics')` inside `analyzer.py`) — a new integration surface. The only in-repo evidence of in-process lizard use is a bare `import lizard` probe in the spike script (`spike2_derisk.py`), which confirms importability but does NOT exercise any lizard Python API (e.g. `lizard.analyze_file`, `lizard.FileInformation`). The plan names no specific lizard API functions/classes it will call in `analyzer.py`, leaving the in-process API surface Partially-verified.
findings.location: AC line 1 (`lizard>=1.23` pip dep); AC line 2 (`guard_import(..., extra="metrics")`); Files: `src/rebar/metrics/analyzer.py`
scenarios: Implementer writes `analyzer.py` calling `lizard.analyze_file(path)` and discovers the return type / field names differ from the CLI output format (e.g. CCN field name), requiring a mid-implementation rework.

If lizard's Python API changed between 1.17 (CLI-era) and 1.23 (pinned floor), the implementer has no spec to anchor against — the version floor `>=1.23` is asserted without citing the specific API stability guarantee.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line API note to the Description or AC2, e.g.: 'calls `lizard.analyze_file(path)` → `FileInformation.average_cyclomatic_complexity`; pin `>=1.23` for the stable `FileInformation` dataclass'. Then add a micro-test in `test_metrics_extra_guard.py` that calls the real API on a known fixture file when the extra is present.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `'lizard'` to the `_HEAVY` ban-list in `tests/unit/test_core_optionality.py` (and document it as the `[metrics]` extra probe), so the existing AST optionality gate actually enforces that `lizard` is never imported at module scope.
evidence: Plan states: 'Test simulating missing extra -> Unavailable; import-linter/optionality check still green.'
_HEAVY tuple in test_core_optionality.py contains: langchain, langgraph, pydantic_ai, tree_sitter, opentelemetry, inspect_ai, anthropic — 'lizard' is absent.
The AST walker in test_no_core_module_imports_heavy_stack_at_module_scope checks only names in _HEAVY; a stray top-level 'import lizard' in analyzer.py would not be caught.
ABSENCE: no reference to 'lizard' found anywhere in tests/unit/test_core_optionality.py
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'import-linter/optionality check still green' as a verification goal, implying the existing AST ban-list gate enforces that `lizard` stays lazy — but `lizard` is NOT in the `_HEAVY` tuple in `tests/unit/test_core_optionality.py`, so a module-scope `import lizard` in the new analyzer would pass the existing gate silently.
findings.location: Verification section: 'import-linter/optionality check still green'; tests/unit/test_core_optionality.py lines 26-46
scenarios: A developer writes `import lizard` at module scope in src/rebar/metrics/analyzer.py; the existing optionality gate passes; a core-only install breaks at import time with ImportError.
The CI 'import-linter' job runs only test_core_optionality.py and test_optional.py — neither checks for lizard, so the gate provides no actual coverage of the new laziness invariant.
findings.suggested_fix: In `tests/unit/test_core_optionality.py`, add `"lizard",` to the `_HEAVY` tuple with a comment: `# [metrics] extra — cyclomatic-complexity analyzer; must stay lazy (inside guard_import call in analyzer.py)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `'metrics'` to the `extra:` matrix in `.github/workflows/_optionality.yml` (the per-extra job) and add `.github/workflows/_optionality.yml` to the plan's 'Files likely touched' list.
evidence: Probed _optionality.yml line 141: `extra: [agents, eval, tracing, mcp, grounding]` — 'metrics' is absent.
The per-extra job installs `nava-rebar[<extra>]`, imports rebar, and asserts `_optional.extra_installed(extra)` — exactly the smoke test the plan's AC3 needs.

The plan's 'Files likely touched' lists only `pyproject.toml, src/rebar/_optional.py, src/rebar/metrics/analyzer.py, tests/unit/metrics/test_metrics_extra_guard.py` — `.github/workflows/_optionality.yml` is absent.
ABSENCE: no mention of CI matrix update anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan adds a new `[metrics]` extra and registers it in `_optional.EXTRAS`, but does not flag that the per-extra CI matrix in `.github/workflows/_optionality.yml` (line 141: `extra: [agents, eval, tracing, mcp, grounding]`) must also be extended to include `metrics`, leaving the per-extra install smoke-test uncovered.
findings.location: .github/workflows/_optionality.yml line 141 (`matrix: extra: [agents, eval, tracing, mcp, grounding]`); plan section 'Acceptance criteria' / 'Files likely touched'
scenarios: The `metrics` extra is shipped; `pip install nava-rebar[metrics]` works locally; but the per-extra CI matrix never runs against it, so a future packaging regression (e.g. a misspelled probe module) would go undetected.
The `_optional.extra_installed('metrics')` path is never exercised in CI, violating the pattern every other extra follows.
findings.suggested_fix: In `_optionality.yml` line 141, change to: `extra: [agents, eval, tracing, mcp, grounding, metrics]`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/test_core_optionality.py` to the files list and add `'lizard'` to its `_HEAVY` tuple so the static AST ban-list enforces the lazy-import contract for the new extra.
evidence: `_HEAVY` tuple in `tests/unit/test_core_optionality.py` lists pydantic_ai, tree_sitter, inspect_ai, opentelemetry etc. but NOT `lizard`
The optionality CI runs `pytest tests/unit/test_core_optionality.py` to enforce this ban-list on every push
Plan does not list `tests/unit/test_core_optionality.py` in 'Files likely touched'
findings.finding: `tests/unit/test_core_optionality.py` is an unenumerated consumer: its `_HEAVY` tuple (the AST ban-list enforced by the optionality CI) does not include `'lizard'`. Adding lizard as an optional pip dep without also adding `'lizard'` to `_HEAVY` leaves the static guard blind to any future accidental module-scope `import lizard`.
scenarios: A future contributor adds `import lizard` at module scope in analyzer.py; the AST walker doesn't catch it because 'lizard' isn't in _HEAVY; the optionality CI stays green falsely.
The plan's own AC says `import rebar[.metrics] stays dependency-free` — the enforcement mechanism for this claim is incomplete without the _HEAVY entry.
findings.suggested_fix: In `tests/unit/test_core_optionality.py`, add `"lizard",` to the `_HEAVY` tuple alongside the existing entries for `tree_sitter` and `pydantic_ai`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `.github/workflows/_optionality.yml` to the files list and append `metrics` to its `per-extra` matrix so the install-and-detect job covers the new extra.
evidence: `.github/workflows/_optionality.yml` line 141: `matrix: extra: [agents, eval, tracing, mcp, grounding]` — `metrics` absent
The per-extra job installs `nava-rebar[$extra]`, imports rebar, and asserts `extra_installed(extra)` — exactly the verification this ticket's AC3 requires
Plan does not list `.github/workflows/_optionality.yml` in 'Files likely touched'
findings.finding: `.github/workflows/_optionality.yml` is an unenumerated consumer: its `per-extra` matrix hard-codes `extra: [agents, eval, tracing, mcp, grounding]` and does not include `metrics`. Every extra registered in `_optional.EXTRAS` has a corresponding matrix entry; omitting `metrics` means the per-extra install-and-detect CI job never runs for this new extra.
scenarios: PR merges; `metrics` extra is registered in EXTRAS and pyproject.toml but the per-extra CI job never installs or probes it — a broken `lizard>=1.23` pin or a misspelled probe module goes undetected until a downstream ticket tries to use the extra.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the matrix line in `_optionality.yml` from `extra: [agents, eval, tracing, mcp, grounding]` to `extra: [agents, eval, tracing, mcp, grounding, metrics]`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `lizard` to `_HEAVY` in `tests/unit/test_core_optionality.py` AND add `metrics` to the `matrix.extra` list in `.github/workflows/_optionality.yml` to close the optionality enforcement loop for the new extra.
evidence: Plan AC line 2: 'import rebar[.metrics] stays dependency-free'
Plan Verification: 'import-linter/optionality check still green'
_optionality.yml matrix.extra lists: [agents, eval, tracing, mcp, grounding] — metrics absent
test_core_optionality.py _HEAVY tuple does not include 'lizard'
Existing pattern: tree_sitter and tree_sitter_language_pack added to _HEAVY when grounding extra was introduced
findings.finding: The plan states 'import-linter/optionality check still green' but does not direct the implementer to add `lizard` to the `_HEAVY` ban-list in `tests/unit/test_core_optionality.py`, nor to add `metrics` to the per-extra CI matrix in `.github/workflows/_optionality.yml`. Without these additions the enforcement mechanism is incomplete: a future accidental module-scope `import lizard` would pass the static AST check silently, and the `extra_installed('metrics')` detection step is never CI-exercised.
scenarios: A future commit adds `import lizard` at module scope in analyzer.py; the static AST check passes silently because lizard is not in _HEAVY, breaking the optionality contract without CI catching it.
The per-extra job never runs `extra_installed('metrics')` detection, so a probe misconfiguration in EXTRAS goes undetected until a user reports a confusing error message.
findings.suggested_fix: In `test_core_optionality.py`, append `"lizard"` to the `_HEAVY` tuple. In `_optionality.yml`, extend `matrix.extra` to include `metrics` so the per-extra install + `extra_installed('metrics')` detection step runs in CI.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the specific `lizard` Python API that `analyzer.py` will call (e.g. `lizard.analyze_file()`, `lizard.get_extensions()`) and confirm `>=1.23` is the version that introduced or stabilized that API surface — add a one-line comment in `pyproject.toml` citing the API and version.
evidence: docs/experiments/code-grounding-spike/spike2_derisk.py only checks `run([sys.executable,'-c','import lizard']).returncode==0` — no version recorded, no API surface validated
Plan asserts `lizard>=1.23` with no citation of the API feature that requires this floor
ABSENCE: no pyproject.toml or spike result records what lizard version was tested or which lizard public API the analyzer.py will invoke
findings.finding: The `lizard>=1.23` version floor is an unverified capability assertion: no in-repo evidence (the spike only checks `import lizard` succeeds, not at what version) records which lizard API the analyzer will call or why >=1.23 is the correct floor.
scenarios: If lizard changed a required API before or at 1.23, the floor could be too low and the analyzer silently produces wrong results on older installs.
If the implementer picks an API that only shipped in e.g. 1.17, the floor is unnecessarily restrictive and will break installs that have a functional older version.
findings.suggested_fix: In pyproject.toml: `"lizard>=1.17"  # lizard.analyze_file() + FunctionInfo.cyclomatic_complexity stable since 1.17; 1.23 adds nothing required here` (or adjust based on actual API audit).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `metrics` to the `per-extra` matrix in `.github/workflows/_optionality.yml` (mirroring the existing `grounding` entry) so the per-extra install + `_optional.extra_installed('metrics')` detection test gates in CI.
evidence: .github/workflows/_optionality.yml line 141: `matrix: extra: [agents, eval, tracing, mcp, grounding]` — metrics is absent
Plan Verification: 'import-linter/optionality check still green' — but per-extra install is the gate that actually proves `pip install nava-rebar[metrics]` resolves cleanly
ABSENCE: plan does not list _optionality.yml in 'Files likely touched'
findings.finding: The plan does not mention adding `metrics` to the `_optionality.yml` per-extra CI matrix (`[agents, eval, tracing, mcp, grounding]`), which is the established enforcement mechanism for per-extra install tests — without this, the 'optionality check still green' claim is not fully covered by prior-art gates.
scenarios: A dependency conflict between lizard and another extra goes undetected until a user reports it post-release.
The per-extra probe in _optional.EXTRAS points to the wrong module name and the CI gap means it's never exercised.
findings.suggested_fix: In _optionality.yml: change `extra: [agents, eval, tracing, mcp, grounding]` to `extra: [agents, eval, tracing, mcp, grounding, metrics]`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `"lizard"` to the `_HEAVY` tuple in `tests/unit/test_core_optionality.py` (and the lean-runtime subprocess check if needed) so module-scope imports of lizard are caught by the existing static guard — add `tests/unit/test_core_optionality.py` to 'Files likely touched'.
evidence: test_core_optionality.py _HEAVY tuple lists: pydantic_ai, tree_sitter, tree_sitter_language_pack, inspect_ai, opentelemetry — lizard is absent
Plan Review-driven corrections: 'Respect the import-linter optionality ban-list' — but the ban-list is the _HEAVY tuple in test_core_optionality.py, not just the AST linter
ABSENCE: 'Files likely touched' does not include tests/unit/test_core_optionality.py
findings.finding: The plan does not mention adding `lizard` to the `_HEAVY` ban-list in `tests/unit/test_core_optionality.py`, which is the static enforcement mechanism that prevents any future module-scope import of heavy extras; without it, the 'import-linter/optionality check still green' AC has no teeth for the lizard module.
findings.location: Verification section: 'import-linter/optionality check still green'; tests/unit/test_core_optionality.py `_HEAVY` tuple
scenarios: A future refactor accidentally hoists `import lizard` to module scope in analyzer.py; since `lizard` is not in _HEAVY, the static guard silently passes and the core gains an eager heavy dep.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `accruing_since` value to pass when constructing `Unavailable` at the `OptionalDependencyError` catch site in the analyzer (e.g., a module-level `_ACCRUING_SINCE` ISO-8601 constant, matching the pattern used in `event_metrics.py` and `sidecar_metrics.py`).
evidence: registry.py line 88-89: `@dataclass(frozen=True) class Unavailable: reason: str; accruing_since: str` — both fields are required, no default
AC2 text: 'missing extra -> OptionalDependencyError caught -> Unavailable(reason names the extra)' — `accruing_since` argument entirely absent
3b30 dep ticket review-driven corrections: 'fallback helper returns Unavailable(reason=..., accruing_since=<ts>)' confirming both fields are required at every construction site
An implementer following AC2 literally will call `Unavailable(reason=...)` and get a `TypeError: missing 1 required positional argument: 'accruing_since'` at the catch site
findings.finding: `Unavailable` requires two fields (`reason`, `accruing_since`) but AC2 only specifies `reason`; the `accruing_since` value to supply at the `OptionalDependencyError` catch site is never defined in the plan, leaving the implementer to guess or produce a broken constructor call.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — AC2: 'Unavailable(reason names the extra)'
scenarios: Core-only install tries `rebar metrics`; the catch block constructs `Unavailable(reason=...)` without `accruing_since`; Python raises `TypeError` — the very crash the AC says must not happen.
Test simulating missing extra calls the guarded path; assertion on `Unavailable` type passes but field access on `result.accruing_since` fails or the constructor already blew up.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2: 'catch site constructs `Unavailable(reason=f"lizard not installed; pip install rebar[metrics]", accruing_since=_ACCRUING_SINCE)` where `_ACCRUING_SINCE` is a module-level ISO-8601 constant in `analyzer.py`.' Mirror the pattern already used in `event_metrics.py` (`accruing_since=_ACCRUING_SINCE`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add concrete proving commands to the Verification section: (a) `pytest tests/unit/metrics/test_metrics_extra_guard.py -v` for AC2; (b) a core-only smoke test such as `pip install -e . && rebar metrics` (or an explicit rationale for skipping it, e.g. 'covered by tox core-only environment in CI'); (c) a grep/install check for AC1, e.g. `grep 'lizard' pyproject.toml && python -c "import rebar.metrics"`
evidence: Verification bullet: 'Test simulating missing extra -> Unavailable; import-linter/optionality check still green' — no pytest path, no CLI invocation, no grep/install command for pyproject.toml
AC3: 'rebar metrics does not crash; code_health reports Unavailable' — user-facing CLI flow; test described as 'simulating' (unit-level mock) with no actual core-only install run
No proving command anchors AC1's EXTRAS dict entry (e.g., grep or pip install -e . check)
findings.finding: The Verification section names the intent ('Test simulating missing extra -> Unavailable; import-linter/optionality check still green') but provides no concrete proving commands, leaving AC1 (EXTRAS registration), AC2 (guard_import path), and AC3 (core-only CLI non-crash) without runnable evidence-producing commands. In particular, AC3's 'rebar metrics does not crash' is a user-facing CLI flow with no end-to-end check (only a simulated/unit test) and no documented rationale for the absence of an actual core-only install smoke test.
scenarios: An agent ships the unit test passing with a mock but never discovers that the actual CLI path still raises ImportError because the catch is mis-scoped
The pyproject.toml extra is mis-spelled ('Lizard' vs 'lizard') and no install-check command catches it
findings.suggested_fix:
Append to Verification:
```
# AC1 — packaging
grep -E 'lizard>=1\.23' pyproject.toml
python -c "from rebar._optional import EXTRAS; assert 'metrics' in EXTRAS"
# AC2 — guard path
pytest tests/unit/metrics/test_metrics_extra_guard.py -v
# AC3 — core-only CLI smoke (or run in tox core-only env)
pip install -e . && rebar metrics   # expect exit 0 with 'code_health: Unavailable'
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix AC line 2 to replace `import rebar[.metrics]` with the explicit Python module paths `import rebar` and `import rebar.metrics`, matching the Description's wording.
evidence: "import rebar[.metrics] stays dependency-free" — square-bracket syntax is a pip extras specifier, not a Python import path
The Description correctly states: "`import rebar` / `import rebar.metrics` must stay dependency-free" using proper module path notation
The AC's verify step references a different entity (rebar[.metrics]) than the Description names (rebar, rebar.metrics), violating the criterion's same-entity requirement
findings.finding: AC line 2 uses the notation `import rebar[.metrics]` which is not valid Python import syntax; it conflates pip-extra notation with Python module import paths, creating ambiguity about exactly which imports must stay dependency-free.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria, line 2: "import rebar[.metrics] stays dependency-free"
scenarios: An implementer reads AC line 2 and is uncertain whether `rebar.metrics.__init__` itself must be import-clean, or only the top-level `rebar` package — the bracketed notation does not clarify this.
findings.suggested_fix: Change "import rebar[.metrics] stays dependency-free" to "both `import rebar` and `import rebar.metrics` stay dependency-free (lizard never imported at package level)"

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale to the Description or Review-driven corrections section explaining why the `guard_import`/`EXTRAS` pattern is the correct approach (e.g., single chokepoint with actionable install hint, consistent with all other extras, enables `extra_installed()` detection).
evidence: Plan Description: 'mirroring the existing grounding/agents extras' — implies alignment but states no affirmative reason.
No sentence in the plan explains WHY guard_import is the correct abstraction boundary for this use case.
Per G6 rubric: 'The plan states a POSITIVE rationale for the chosen approach (its absence is a finding)'
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for the chosen `guard_import`/`EXTRAS` approach — it describes what to implement but not why this mechanism is the right fit (e.g., why a single chokepoint with an install hint is preferred over a try/except capability flag or a thin always-present stub).
scenarios: An implementer reading this plan without knowing the existing extras pattern may choose an ad-hoc try/except at module level, violating the architectural contract.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
